title: Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita

Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot displays the relationship between the share of workers in informal employment and GDP per capita for various countries in 2024. The vertical axis represents the percentage of informal employment, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis shows GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale from roughly $1,000 to over $100,000. A general negative trend is visible, where countries with lower GDP per capita tend to have higher shares of informal employment. In the lower income range, between $2,000 and $5,000, countries such as the Democratic Republic of Congo, Chad, Tanzania, Ethiopia, and Somalia cluster in the upper bands, with informal employment shares generally between 80% and 100%. India appears as a large circle in the $10,000 to $20,000 range with an informal employment share near 90%, while Indonesia and Ghana sit slightly lower in the 80% to 90% band. Moving toward the $20,000 mark, countries like Thailand, Mexico, and Argentina show shares roughly between 40% and 60%. In the higher income bands, above $50,000, countries including Spain, Germany, and South Korea cluster near the bottom of the chart, with informal employment shares generally below 20%. The size of the circles corresponds to population, with India and Indonesia represented by the largest markers. The data includes the production of goods for own use, such as subsistence farming, in the definition of employment, and GDP figures are expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices.
